findings.description: Path traversal / arbitrary file write: filename is validated only by extension (os.path.splitext keeps '../' components), so a payload like '../../etc/cron.d/evil.pdf' passes the ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS check and os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, filename) writes outside UPLOAD_DIR, enabling overwrite of arbitrary files (CWE-22/CWE-434, OWASP A01/A03).

Path traversal via unvalidated upload_id interpolated into the chunk filename (f"{upload_id}.{index}"); an upload_id such as '../../etc/cron.d/x' makes receive_chunk write attacker-controlled bytes outside CHUNK_DIR (CWE-22, OWASP A01); same tainted upload_id is reused at lines 66 and 76.

Unbounded resource consumption: per-chunk 'data' is written with no size cap and there is no aggregate limit, while MAX_FILE_MB / file_size_ok (line 90) are never invoked anywhere in the upload path, so an attacker can exhaust disk via arbitrarily large chunks/chunk counts (CWE-400/CWE-770, OWASP A04).

verify_checksum reads the entire stored file into memory via f.read() with no size guard (and MAX_FILE_MB is unenforced), allowing memory-exhaustion DoS on large uploads; should stream the hash in fixed-size blocks (CWE-400).

open(dest_path, 'wb') follows symlinks and unconditionally truncates an existing target; combined with attacker-influenced filename and a predictable UPLOAD_DIR, this permits targeted overwrite of a symlinked/pre-existing file (CWE-59); depends on local filesystem state hence lower confidence.

Checksum comparison uses non-constant-time '!=' on digest strings (CWE-208); low impact here since a SHA-256 of client content is not a guess-protected secret, but constant-time comparison (hmac.compare_digest) is the hardened idiom.

Off-by-one: range(session.total_chunks - 1) omits the final chunk (index total_chunks-1), so assemble() silently produces a truncated/corrupted file; primarily a logic concern (defer detail to sibling critic) but flagged for data-integrity impact.
